caption:
Filing your ITR before July 31? These 3 mistakes are why lakhs of Indians get income tax notices every year — and most don't see them coming.

The IT department's Annual Information Statement (AIS) has your complete financial picture: FD interest, dividends, stock sales, rental income — all reported directly by banks and institutions. If your ITR shows less than what your AIS records, their system auto-flags it. No human needed to catch you.

The sneakiest miss is bank interest. Forget to declare ₹45,000 in FD interest + ₹8,000 savings interest = ₹53,000 unreported. At a 30% slab with cess, that's ~₹16,500 in extra tax demand — plus Section 234B interest on top. (Illustrative numbers only.)

And the wrong ITR form? ITR-1 is only for simple salaried income under ₹50L. Freelancers, business owners, and anyone with capital gains or foreign income needs a different form. File the wrong one and you get a defective return notice — and start over.

What to do: Log in to incometax.gov.in → AIS → download and cross-check every entry before you hit Submit.

Not sure what applies to your case? WhatsApp us — we'll check your AIS and file it right the first time.

Note: general information, not professional advice — talk to us for your specific case.

Source: Income Tax Act 1961; AIS framework under Section 285BB.

slides.headline: 3 ITR mistakes that bring a tax notice to your door

slides.label: ITR
slides.headline: Mistake #1 — the IT dept already knows your income
slides.body: The Annual Information Statement (AIS) pulls data from banks, mutual funds, employers, brokers, and property registrars. Every interest credit, dividend, and asset sale is logged there. If your ITR income is lower than what the AIS shows, an automated flag goes up — no human required to catch the mismatch.

slides.label: ITR
slides.headline: Mistake #2 — ignoring bank interest
slides.body: FD interest and savings account interest are taxable income, and your bank reports every rupee of them to the IT department. Most people forget to include these — especially across multiple bank accounts. Even ₹30,000–₹50,000 in unreported interest can generate a demand notice within months of filing.

slides.label: ITR
slides.headline: Mistake #3 — filing the wrong ITR form
slides.body: ITR-1 is only for salaried individuals with income up to ₹50L and no capital gains or business income. Freelancers need ITR-3 or ITR-4. Anyone with foreign assets, rental income, or stock gains needs a different form too. Wrong form = defective return notice = you file all over again.

slides.label: ITR
slides.body: Illustration: Ravi earns ₹10L in salary. He skips declaring ₹45,000 FD interest + ₹8,000 savings interest = ₹53,000 unreported. At a 30% slab with 4% cess, that's ~₹16,500 in extra tax demand — before Section 234B interest is added. One AIS cross-check, fifteen minutes, would have caught all of it. (Illustrative numbers — not a guarantee.)

slides.label: ITR
slides.title: Your pre-filing checklist
points: Download your AIS from incometax.gov.in and match every line item against your own records before you file.
Add up FD interest and savings account interest from every bank account you hold — all of it is reportable.
Confirm you are using the correct ITR form for your income type; when in doubt, ask a CA.
Declare capital gains on stocks and mutual funds, even if the total falls within the ₹1.25L LTCG exempt limit.
File before July 31 — early filing gives you time to spot, correct, and resubmit without penalties.
